text: The robotic surgical setup for a partial nephrectomy is displayed, showing the camera port, two working ports, a port for retraction using the pro grasp, two assistant ports, and a port for liver retraction. Imaging reveals a 3 to 3.5 cm right upper pole renal mass adjacent to the liver.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the ascending colon, followed by the careful caucarization of the duodenum using diathermy. The gonadal vein is then medialized, and the ureter along with the lower pole of the kidney is lifted.

The surgeon dissects parallel to the inferior vena cava, carefully approaching the hilar structures. The renal artery is identified, dissected, and looped with a vessel loop, followed by the dissection and looping of the renal vein.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the renal upper pole to access the tumor. The kidney is mobilized, and the serotus fascia is opened to prepare for tumor resection. Sticky fat surrounding the tumor is carefully resected, ensuring the tumor is not breached.

The surgeon uses diathermy to score the renal capsule, marking the resection plane. A separate renal artery supplying the upper pole is identified and looped at the hilum. Bulldog clamps are applied to the renal arteries and vein. Tumor resection begins with diathermy and gentle manipulation to push the tumor away from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon enters the collecting system while ensuring a good margin is developed around the tumor. The lower margin is excised and sent for frozen section analysis to confirm clear margins.

The surgeon begins the first layer of renal roughy using B-lock 3-0 sutures in a continuous fashion, securing each end with hemo lock clips. The sutures are brought through the renal capsule and secured. Early unclamping of the renal vessels is performed, with a noted warm ischemia time of twenty minutes.

After early unclamping, the surgeon proceeds with the second layer closure using V-lock 2-0 sutures and the sliding clip method. Vessel loops are retracted, and flow sealant is applied to the area. The resected tumor is placed in a bag, and a drain is inserted. The final view shows the bivalved resected specimen, a drain, and multiple small incisions, including a larger incision approximately three centimeters for specimen retrieval.
